content: A standard penetration test (SPT) was carried out at a location by using a manually operated hammer dropping system with 50% efficiency. The recorded SPT value at a particular depth is 28. If an automatic hammer dropping system with 70% efficiency is used at the same location, the recorded SPT value will be _________.
explanation:
A Standard Penetration Test (SPT) is used in geotechnical investigations to determine the resistance of soil to penetration by a standard sampler. The efficiency of the hammer system used during the test can significantly affect the recorded SPT values. To compare the SPT values obtained with different hammer efficiencies, we use a correction factor.

The formula to adjust the recorded SPT value is :

$$ N_{cor} = N_{field} \times \frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} $$

where:

  • $ N_{cor} $ = Corrected SPT value
  • $ N_{field} $ = Recorded SPT value in the field
  • $ E_{1} $ = Efficiency of the new hammer system
  • $ E_{2} $ = Efficiency of the existing hammer system

In this case, the original SPT value ($ N_{field} $) is recorded with a hammer system having 50% efficiency, and we need to find the equivalent SPT value if the system is replaced by an automatic hammer with 70% efficiency.

Substituting the values into the formula :

$$ N_{cor} = 28 \times \frac{50}{70} $$

First, simplify the fraction :

$$ \frac{50}{70} = \frac{5}{7} \approx 0.714 $$

Now, multiply by the recorded SPT value :

$$ N_{cor} = 28 \times 0.714 \approx 20 $$

Thus, the corrected SPT value when using an automatic hammer with 70% efficiency will be approximately 20.

Therefore, the correct option is :

Option B : 20

content:
Read the following statements :

(P) While designing a shallow footing in sandy soil, monsoon season is considered for critical design in terms of bearing capacity.

(Q) For slope stability of an earthen dam, sudden drawdown is never a critical condition.

(R) In a sandy sea beach, quicksand condition can arise only if the critical hydraulic gradient exceeds the existing hydraulic gradient.

(S) The active earth thrust on a rigid retaining wall supporting homogenous cohesionless backfill will reduce with the lowering of water table in the backfill.

Which one of the following combinations is correct?
explanation:
The stability of a slope is greatly affected by the sudden drawdown condition; this is one of the primary causes of slope failure.

Active earth thrust decreases when the water table is lowered.

For example :

When the water table (WT) is at ground level (GL), the active pressure at the base is $k_a \gamma' H + \gamma_w H$.

When there is no water table (WT), the active thrust at the base is $k_a \gamma H$.

A quicksand condition will not occur if the critical hydraulic gradient ($i_c$) is greater than the existing hydraulic gradient ($i$).

content:
At a site, Static Cone Penetration Test was carried out. The measured point (tip) resistance qc was 1000 kPa at a certain depth. The friction ratio (fr) was estimated as 1% at the same depth.

The value of sleeve (side) friction (in kPa) at that depth was __________. (in integer)
explanation:
Friction ratio, $${f_r} = {{{q_s}} \over {{q_t}}} = 1\% $$

qs = Sleeve friction

st = Tip friction

$${{{q_s}} \over {{q_t}}} = {1 \over {100}}$$ (qt = 1000 kPa)

$${q_s} = {{1000} \over {100}} = 10$$ kPa

content:
A designer used plate load test to obtain the value of the bearing capacity factor $\mathrm{N}_\gamma$. A circular plate of 1 m diameter was placed on the surface of a dry sand layer extending very deep beneath the ground. The unit weight of the sand is $16.66 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The plate is loaded to failure at a pressure of 1500 kPa .

Considering Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory, the bearing capacity factor $\mathrm{N}_\gamma$ is________ (round off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
To determine the bearing capacity factor $\mathrm{N}_\gamma$ for the circular plate using Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory, we begin with the following formula for the ultimate bearing capacity $q_u$:

Circular Plate Bearing Capacity Equation:

$ q_u = 1.3 \cdot C \cdot N_C + \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot N_q + 0.3 \cdot B \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma $

Where:

$C$ is the cohesion of the soil (For sand, $C = 0$).

$\gamma$ is the unit weight of the soil ($16.66 \, \text{kN/m}^3$ for the given sand).

$D_f$ is the depth of the foundation (for this problem $D_f = 0$, meaning the plate is on the ground surface).

$B$ is the diameter of the plate (1 m for this problem).

$N_C$, $N_q$, and $N_\gamma$ are the bearing capacity factors.

Substitute Values for Sand:

Since $C = 0$ and $D_f = 0$, the equation simplifies to:

$ q_u = 0.3 \cdot 1 \cdot 16.66 \cdot N_\gamma $

We know that $q_u = 1500 \, \text{kPa}$ (the pressure at failure):

$ 0.3 \cdot 16.66 \cdot N_\gamma = 1500 $

Solve for $\mathrm{N}_\gamma$:

$ 0.3 \cdot 16.66 \cdot N_\gamma = 1500 $

$ N_\gamma = \frac{1500}{0.3 \times 16.66} \approx 300.12 $

Rounding to the nearest integer:

$ N_\gamma \approx 300 $

Thus, the bearing capacity factor $\mathrm{N}_\gamma$ is approximately 300.

content:
A $6 \mathrm{~m} \times 6 \mathrm{~m}$ square footing constructed in clay is subjected to a vertical load of 2500 kN at its centre. The base of the footing is 2 m below the ground surface, as shown in the figure. The footing is made of 2 m thick concrete. The ground water table is at a great depth. Considering Terzaghi's bearing capacity theory, the factor of safety of footing against the bearing capacity failure is _________ (rounded off to 2 decimal places). Note:

Unit of concrete $=24 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^3$

Properties of clay: $c=50 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^3, \phi=0^{\circ}$, and $\gamma=19 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^3$

For $\phi=0^{\circ}: N_c=5.7, N_q=1, N_\gamma=0$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text {safe }} & =\frac{Q_u-\bar{\sigma}}{F O S}+\bar{\sigma} \\ & =\frac{Q_u-\gamma D_f}{F O S}+\gamma D_f \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { } \begin{aligned} Here,\,\,\,\text { Applied load } & =\frac{2500}{6 \times 6}+\gamma_c \times 2 \\ & =66.67+24 \times 2=117.44 \mathrm{kN} / \mathrm{m}^2 \\ \text { For square footing, } Q_u & =1.3 \mathrm{CN}_c+\gamma D_f N_q+0.4 B \gamma N_r \\ & =1.3 \times 50 \times 5.7+19 \times 2 \times 1+0 \\ & =370.5+38=408.5 \\ \Rightarrow \quad Q_{\text {safe }} & =\frac{408.5-38}{F O S}+38 \\ 117.44 & =\frac{370.5}{F O S}+38 \\ F O S & =4.66 \end{aligned} $$

content:
A square footing is to be designed to carry a column load of 500 kN which is resting on a soil stratum having the following average properties: bulk unit weight = 19 kN/m3; angle of internal friction = 0° and cohesion = 25 kPa. Considering the depth of the footing as 1 m and adopting Meyerhof’s bearing capacity theory with a factor of safety of 3, the width of the footing (in m) is _________ (round off to one decimal place)

[Assume the applicable shape and depth factor values as unity; ground water level at greater depth.]
explanation:
Given data :

  • Load from column (Qs) = 500 kN
  • Bulk unit weight (γ) = 19 kN/m3
  • Angle of internal friction (φ) = 0°
  • Cohesion (C) = 25 kPa
  • Depth of footing (Df) = 1 m
  • Factor of Safety (FOS) = 3

According to Meyerhof's bearing capacity formula :

$$ q_u = C N_c S_c d_c i_c + \bar{\sigma} N_q S_q d_q i_q + \frac{1}{2} B \gamma N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma i_\gamma $$

Given φ = 0°:

  • Nc = 5.14
  • Nq = 1
  • Nγ = 0

Substituting Nc, Nq, and Nγ into the equation :

$$ q_u = 25 \times 5.14 + (19) \times 1 + 0 $$

$$ q_u = 147.5 \text{ kPa} $$

Net ultimate bearing capacity (qnu) :

$$ q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f $$

$$ q_{nu} = 147.5 - 19 $$

$$ q_{nu} = 128.5 \text{ kPa} $$

Net safe bearing capacity (qns) :

$$ q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{FOS} $$

$$ q_{ns} = \frac{128.5}{3} $$

$$ q_{ns} = 42.83 \text{ kPa} $$

Given total safe load (Qns) = 500 kN and using the relationship :

$$ Q_{ns} = q_{ns} \times \text{Area} $$

$$ Q_{ns} = q_{ns} \times B^2 $$

Substituting the values :

$$ 500 = 42.83 \times B^2 $$

$$ B = \sqrt{\frac{500}{42.83}} $$

$$ B = 3.41 \text{ m} $$

Therefore, the width of the footing is 3.4 meters (rounded to one decimal place).

content:
A circular pile of diameter 0.6 m and length 8 m was constructed in a cohesive soil stratum having the following properties: bulk unit weight = 19 kN/m3; angle of internal friction = 0° and cohesion = 25 kPa.

The allowable load the pile can carry with a factor of safety of 3 is __________ kN (round off to one decimal place).

[Adopt: Adhesion factor, α = 1.0 and Bearing capacity factor, Nc = 9.0]
explanation:
To determine the allowable load the pile can carry, we need to calculate both the skin friction and the end bearing resistance. Then, we'll apply the factor of safety to find the allowable load.

The pile is described with the following attributes:

  • Diameter, $ D = 0.6 $ m
  • Length, $ L = 8 $ m
  • Bulk unit weight, $ \gamma = 19 $ kN/m3
  • Angle of internal friction, $ \phi = 0^\circ $
  • Cohesion, $ c = 25 $ kPa
  • Adhesion factor, $ \alpha = 1.0 $
  • Bearing capacity factor, $ N_c = 9.0 $
  • Factor of Safety, $ FS = 3 $

The ultimate load $ Q_u $ the pile can carry is the sum of the skin friction resistance $ Q_s $ and the end bearing resistance $ Q_p $.

Skin Friction Resistance $ Q_s $

The skin friction resistance is given by the formula:

$$ Q_s = \alpha \cdot A_s \cdot c $$

where:

  • $ A_s $ = Surface area of the pile in contact with the soil
  • $ A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L $

Substituting the given values:

$$ A_s = \pi \cdot 0.6 \, \text{m} \cdot 8 \, \text{m} = 15.08 \, \text{m}^2 $$

Then:

$$ Q_s = 1.0 \cdot 15.08 \, \text{m}^2 \cdot 25 \, \text{kPa} = 377 \, \text{kN} $$

End Bearing Resistance $ Q_p $

The end bearing resistance is given by:

$$ Q_p = N_c \cdot A_p \cdot c $$

where:

  • $ A_p $ = Cross-sectional area of the pile
  • $ A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 $

Substituting the given values:

$$ A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.6 \, \text{m})^2 = 0.2827 \, \text{m}^2 $$

Then:

$$ Q_p = 9.0 \cdot 0.2827 \, \text{m}^2 \cdot 25 \, \text{kPa} = 63.6 \, \text{kN} $$

Total Ultimate Load $ Q_u $

The ultimate load is the sum of skin friction and end bearing resistance:

$$ Q_u = Q_s + Q_p = 377 \, \text{kN} + 63.6 \, \text{kN} = 440.6 \, \text{kN} $$

Allowable Load $ Q_{allowable} $

Applying the factor of safety:

$$ Q_{allowable} = \frac{Q_u}{FS} = \frac{440.6 \, \text{kN}}{3} \approx 146.9 \, \text{kN} $$

Thus, the allowable load the pile can carry is approximately 146.9 kN (rounded off to one decimal place).

content: For the flow setup shown in the figure (not to scale), the hydraulic conductivities of the two soil samples, Soil 1 and Soil 2, are 10 mm/s and 1 mm/s, respectively. Assume the unit weight of water as 10 kN/m3 and ignore the velocity head. At steady state, what is the total head (in m, rounded off to two decimal places) at any point located at the junction of the two samples? _____
explanation:
The head due to an external pressure of 10 kPa is calculated as follows :

Head, $ h_w $ = $ \frac{10 \text{kPa}}{10 \text{kN}/\text{m}^3} $

$$ h_w = 1 \text{ m} $$

The total head at point P is :

$$ \text{Total Head at P} = \text{Elevation head} + \text{Pressure head} $$

$$ (TH)_P = 3 + 1 + h_w = 3 + 1 + 1 = 5 \text{ m} $$

Now, calculating the total head at junction Q :

$$ (TH)_Q = (TH)_P - h_{L1} $$

The discharge, Q, is given by :

$$ Q = k_{eq} \times i \times A $$

Where $ k_{eq} $ is the equivalent hydraulic conductivity. Combining Soil 1 and Soil 2 :

$$ k_{eq} = \frac{L1 + L2}{\frac{L1}{k1} + \frac{L2}{k2}} $$

$$ Q = \left[\frac{1 + 1}{\left(\frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{1}\right)}\right] \times \frac{5}{2} \times A $$

$$ Q = 4.545 \text{ A} \quad .......(i) $$

The discharge through Soil 1 is :

$$ Q = k1 \times i1 \times A = 10 \times \left(\frac{h_{L1}}{L1}\right) \times A $$

$$ Q = 10 \times \left(\frac{h_{L1}}{1}\right) \times A \quad ......(ii) $$

Substituting Q from equations (i) and (ii) :

$$ 4.545 A = 10 \times \frac{h_{L1}}{1} \times A $$

$$ h_{L1} = 0.4545 $$

Thus, the total head at junction Q is :

$$ (TH)_Q = (TH)_P - h_{L1} $$

$$ (TH)_Q = 5 - 0.4545 $$

$$ (TH)_Q = 4.54 \text{ m} $$

Hence, the total head at junction Q is 4.54 meters.

content:
A square footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m is placed 1.0 m below the ground surface on a cohesionless homogeneous soil stratum. Considering that the groundwater table is located at the base of the footing, the unit weights of soil above and below the groundwater table are 18 kN/m3 and 20 kN/m3, respectively, and the bearing capacity factor Nq is 58, the net ultimate bearing capacity of the soil is estimated as 1706 kPa (unit weight of water = 10 kN/m3).

Earlier, a plate load test was carried out with a circular plate of 30 cm diameter in the same foundation pit during a dry season, when the water table was located beyond the plate influence zone. Using Terzaghi’s bearing capacity formulation, what is the ultimate bearing capacity (in kPa) of the plate?
explanation:
Given parameters :

  • $\gamma_t = 18 \mathrm{kN}/\mathrm{m}^3$
  • $\gamma_{s a t} = 20 \mathrm{kN}/\mathrm{m}^3$
  • $q_{n u} = 1706 \mathrm{kPa}$
  • $\gamma_w = 10 \mathrm{kN}/\mathrm{m}^3$
  • $N_q = 58$

From the plate load test, we have :

Diameter of the plate, $(d)=30 \mathrm{~cm}$

The ultimate bearing capacity, $q_{m u}$, is given by :

$$ q_{m u} = q (N_q - 1) + 0.4 B \gamma N_\gamma $$

Substitute the given values :

$$ q_{m u} = 18 \times 1 \times (58 - 1) + 0.4 \times 2.5 \times (20 - 10) \times N_\gamma $$

Calculate $N_\gamma$ as follows :

$$ N_\gamma = \frac{(1706 - 18 \times 57)}{(0.4 \times 2.5 \times 10)} \Rightarrow N_\gamma = 68 $$

The ultimate bearing capacity of the plate, $q_{\text{uplate}}$, is :

$$ q_{\text{uplate}} = 0.3 \times d \times \gamma_t \times N_\gamma $$

Substitute the values :

$$ q_{\text{uplate}} = 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 18 \times 68 = 110.16 \mathrm{kPa} $$

Thus, the ultimate bearing capacity of the plate is 110.16 kPa.
